name: BPF
doc: A band pass filter permits the frequencies around a specified centre frequency to pass unaltered through the filter while the frequencies either side are attenuated. The frequencies that pass through are known as the bandwidth or the band pass of the filter. Used to create timbres consisting of fizzy harmonics, lo-fi qualities or very thin sounds that may form the basis of sound effects.

argDocs.rq: the reciprocal of Q. bandwidth / cutoffFreq

name: BPZ2
doc: two zero fixed midpass which cuts out 0 Hz and the Nyquist frequency. Implements the formula: out(i) = 0.5 * (in(i) - in(i-2))

name: BRF
summary: second order Butterworth band reject filter
doc: Band reject filters, also known as notch filters, attenuate a selected range of frequencies effectively creating a notch in the sound. This type of filter is handy for scooping out frequencies, thinning out a sound while leaving the fundamental intact, making them useful for creating timbres that contain a discernable pitch but do not have a high level of harmonic content.

argDocs.rq: the reciprocal of Q. bandwidth / cutoffFreq

name: BRZ2
doc: two zero fixed midcut which cuts out frequencies around 1/2 of the Nyquist frequency. Implements the formula: out(i) = 0.5 * (in(i) + in(i-2))

name: Decay
doc: This is essentially the same as integrator except that instead of supplying the coefficient directly, it is calculated from a 60 dB decay time. This is the time required for the integrator to lose 99.9 % of its value or -60dB. This is useful for exponential decaying envelopes triggered by impulses.

name: Decay2
doc: triggered exponential attack and exponential decay. Decay has a very sharp attack and can produce clicks. Decay2 rounds off the attack by subtracting one Decay from another. (decay in attack-time decay-time) equivalent to: (- (decay in attack-time decay-time) (decay in attack-time decay-time))

name: DetectSilence
doc: If the signal input starts with silence at the beginning of the synth's duration, then DetectSilence will wait indefinitely until the first sound before starting to monitor for silence. This UGen outputs 1 if silence is detected, otherwise 0.

argDocs.amp: when input falls below this, evaluate done action
argDocs.time: the minimum duration of the input signal which input must fall below thresh before this triggers. The default is 0.1 seconds
argDocs.action: the action to perform when silence is detected. Default: NO-ACTION

name: Formlet
doc: a resonant filter whose impulse response is like that of a sine wave with a Decay2 envelope over it. The great advantage to this filter over FOF is that there is no limit to the number of overlapping grains since the grain is just the impulse response of the filter. Note that if attacktime == decaytime then the signal cancels out and if attacktime > decaytime then the impulse response is inverted.

name: FOS
doc: first order filter section. Formula is equivalent to: out(i) = (a0 * in(i)) + (a1 * in(i-1)) + (b1 * out(i-1))

name: HPF
doc: A high pass filter lets through the frequencies above the cutoff point and successfully dampens the frequencies below the cutoff point. This effectively removes the fundamental frequency of the sound, leaving only the fizz harmonic overtones. High pass filters are rarely used in the creation of instruments and are predominantly used to create effervexcent sound effects of bright timbres that can be laid over the top of another low pass sound to increase the harmonic content.

name: HPZ1
doc: two point difference filter. Implements the formula: out(i) = 0.5 * (in(i) - in(i-1))

name: HPZ2
doc: two zero fixed highpass. Implements the formula: out(i) = 0.25 * (in(i) - (2*in(i-1)) + in(i-2))

name: Integrator
doc: leaky integrator. Integrates an input signal with a leak. The formula implemented is: out(0) = in(0) + (coef * out(-1))

name: Lag
doc: exponential lag, useful for smoothing out control signals. This is essentially the same as OnePole except that instead of supplying the coefficient directly, it is calculated from a 60 dB lag time. This is the time required for the filter to converge to within 0.01 % of a value.

name: Lag2
doc: equivalent to (lag (lag in time) time), resulting in a smoother transition. This saves on CPU as you only have to calculate the decay factor once instead of twice. See lag for more details.

name: Lag2UD
doc: equivalent to (lag-ud (lag-ud in up-t down-t) up-t down-t) thus resulting in a smoother transition. This saves on CPU as you only have to calculate the decay factor once instead of twice. See Lag for more details.

argDocs.lagTimeUp: 60 dB lag time in seconds for the upgoing signal
argDocs.lagTimeDown: 60 dB lag time in seconds for the downgoing signal

name: Lag3
doc: lag3 is equivalent to (lag (lag (lag in time) time) time), thus resulting in a smoother transition. This saves on CPU as you only have to calculate the decay factor once instead of three times. See Lag for more details.

name: Lag3UD
doc: equivalent to (lag-ud (lag-ud (lag-ud (in up-t down-t) up-t down-t) up-t, down-t) thus resulting in a smoother transition. This saves on CPU as you only have to calculate the decay factor once instead of three times.

argDocs.lagTimeUp: 60 dB lag time in seconds for the upgoing signal
argDocs.lagTimeDown: 60 dB lag time in seconds for the downgoing signal

name: LagUD
doc: the same as Lag except that you can supply a different 60 dB time for when the signal goes up, from when the signal goes down

argDocs.lagTimeUp: 60 dB lag time in seconds for the upgoing signal
argDocs.lagTimeDown: 60 dB lag time in seconds for the downgoing signal

name: LeakDC
doc: removes a DC offset from signal. For example, a square wave contains prolonged sections of the cycle which are at +1 and -1 (the top and bottom of the square sections). If you were to pass this wave through leak-dc, then these top parts would taper back towards 0 with a greater slope as you move coef from 1 to 0.. Good starting point coef values are to 0.995 for audio rate and 0.9 for control rate

argDocs.coef: leak coefficient. A value of 1 indicates no leakage and 0 indicates high leakage - essentially the rate at which the offset will return back to 0

name: LPF
summary: second order Butterworth low pass filter
doc: A low pass filter is a standard subtractive synthesis tool which removes frequencies above a defined cut-off point. This typically has the effect of making bright sounds duller. Using a low pass filter allows you to have fine-grained control of the level of brightness/dullness to tune your timbre in addition to allowing you to modulate the effect in real time thus creating movement in the sound.

name: LPZ1
doc: two point average filter. Implements the formula: out(i) = 0.5 * (in(i) + in(i-1))

name: LPZ2
doc: two zero fixed lowpass. Implements the formula: out(i) = 0.25 * (in(i) + (2*in(i-1)) + in(i-2))

name: Median
doc: returns the median of the last length input points. This non linear filter is good at reducing impulse noise from a signal.

argDocs.length: number of input points in which to find the median. Must be an odd number from 1 to 31. If length is 1 then Median has no effect.

name: MidEQ
doc: attenuates or boosts a frequency band

argDocs.freq: center frequency of the band in Hertz
argDocs.rq: the reciprocal of Q. bandwidth / cutoffFreq
argDocs.db: amount of boost (db > 0) or attenuation (db < 0) of the frequency band

name: OnePole
doc: A one pole filter. Implements the formula: out(i) = ((1 - abs(coef)) * in(i)) + (coef * out(i-1))

argDocs.coef: feedback coefficient. Should be between -1 and +1

name: OneZero
doc: A one zero filter. Implements the formula : out(i) = ((1 - abs(coef)) * in(i)) + (coef * in(i-1))

argDocs.coef: feed forward coefficient. +0.5 makes a two point averaging filter (see also lpz1), -0.5 makes a differentiator (see also hpz1), +1 makes a single sample delay (see also delay1), -1 makes an inverted single sample delay.

name: Ramp
doc: similar to lag but with a linear rather than exponential lag, useful for smoothing out control signals

name: Resonz
doc: A Note on Constant-Gain Digital Resonators,\" Computer Music Journal, vol 18, no. 4, pp. 8-10, Winter 1994.\" Computer Music Journal, vol 18, no. 4, pp. 8-10, Winter 1994.

argDocs.bwr: bandwidth ratio (reciprocal of Q). rq = bandwidth / centerFreq

name: RHPF
doc: A resonant high pass filter lets through the frequencies above the cutoff point and successfully dampens the frequencies below the cutoff point. This effectively removes the fundamental frequency of the sound, leaving only the fizz harmonic overtones. However, in addition to this behaviour, the resonant high pass filter also emphasises/resonates the frequencies around the cutoff point. The amount of emphasis is controlled by the rq param with a lower rq resulting in greater resonance. High amounts of resonance (rq ~0) can create a whistling sound around the cutoff frequency. High pass filters are rarely used in the creation of instruments and are predominantly used to create effervescent sound effects of bright timbres that can be laid over the top of another low pass sound to increase the harmonic content.

argDocs.rq: the reciprocal of Q. bandwidth / cutoffFreq. A lower rq results in more resonance

name: Ringz
doc: Ringz is the same as Resonz, except that instead of a resonance parameter, the bandwidth is specified in a 60dB ring decay time. One Ringz is equivalent to one component of the klank ugen

argDocs.decayTime: the 60 dB decay time of the filter

name: RLPF
doc: A resonant low pass filter is a standard subtractive synthesis tool which removes frequencies above a defined cut-off point. This typically has the effect of making bright sounds duller. However, in addition to this behaviour, the resonant low pass filter also emphasises/resonates the frequencies around the cutoff point. The amount of emphasis is controlled by the rq param with a lower rq resulting in greater resonance. High amounts of resonance (rq ~0) can create a whistling sound around the cutoff frequency. Using a low pass filter allows you to have fine-grained control of the level of brightness/dullness to tune your timbre in addition to allowing you to modulate the effect in real time thus creating movement in the sound.

argDocs.rq: the reciprocal of Q. bandwidth / cutoffFreq. A lower rq results in more resonance.

name: Slew
doc: Smooth the curve by limiting the slope of the input signal to up and dn

name: Slope
doc: Measures the rate of change per second of a signal. Formula implemented is: out[i] = (in[i] - in[i-1]) * sampling_rate

name: SOS
doc: second order filter section (biquad). Formula is equivalent to: out(i) = (a0 * in(i)) + (a1 * in(i-1)) + (a2 * in(i-2)) + (b1 * out(i-1)) + (b2 * out(i-2))

name: TwoPole
doc: a two pole filter. This provides lower level access to setting of pole location. For general purposes Resonz is better.

argDocs.radius: radius of pole. Should be between 0 and +1

name: TwoZero
doc: a two zero filter
